questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of material culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A large coordinated campaign pressing lawmakers for new environmental protections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A nation celebrating many groups' festivals and languages rather than promoting a single culture.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a newly invented communication technology.

questions.explanation: An example of material culture is: the smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society. In general, material culture refers to the physical objects and artifacts that members of a society create and use. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: globalization

choices.label: B
choices.text: acculturation

choices.label: C
choices.text: ethnocentrism

choices.label: D
choices.text: stigma

questions.explanation: This description matches ethnocentrism, which is the tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes assimilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The view that deviance arises when people cannot reach valued goals through legitimate means.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The process by which societies move toward industrial, urban, and rationalized forms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Widespread cultural patterns that are accessible to most members of a society.

questions.explanation: Assimilation is best described as the process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of mores?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A movement gaining momentum only after securing funding, volunteers, and press coverage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intense public outrage directed at someone who defrauds a vulnerable elderly person.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A student cheating once without ever coming to see themselves as a cheater.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A graduation ceremony with caps, gowns, and the turning of tassels.

questions.explanation: An example of mores is: intense public outrage directed at someone who defrauds a vulnerable elderly person. In general, mores refers to strongly held norms with moral significance whose violation brings serious condemnation. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes labeling theory?

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a social movement?

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of Weber's law?

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes social facilitation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The gap between what a learner can do alone and what they can do with guidance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Improved performance on easy or well-learned tasks in the presence of others.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tendency to favor the members of one's own group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Kohlberg's level of moral reasoning based on social approval and upholding laws.

questions.explanation: Social facilitation is best described as improved performance on easy or well-learned tasks in the presence of others. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of diffusion of responsibility?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each witness feeling less obligated to act because responsibility feels shared.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A tutor giving hints that are gradually withdrawn as the student improves.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Defending a choice by appeal to universal justice over a written law.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rating one's own team's work more highly simply because it is one's own.

questions.explanation: An example of diffusion of responsibility is: each witness feeling less obligated to act because responsibility feels shared. In general, diffusion of responsibility refers to the reduced sense of personal responsibility felt when others are present. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: increased liking for a stimulus that results simply from repeated exposure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the mere exposure effect

choices.label: B
choices.text: in-group bias

choices.label: C
choices.text: the actor-observer bias

choices.label: D
choices.text: avoidant attachment

questions.explanation: This description matches the mere exposure effect, which is increased liking for a stimulus that results simply from repeated exposure. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes peripheral route persuasion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A style high in warmth but low in demands and limits.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An insecure bond in which an infant avoids or ignores the caregiver.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Attitude change driven by superficial cues rather than by argument quality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attributing one's own actions to the situation but others' actions to their character.

questions.explanation: Peripheral route persuasion is best described as attitude change driven by superficial cues rather than by argument quality. The other options describe different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes drive-reduction theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The view that people are motivated to maintain an optimal level of arousal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The view that motivation thrives on autonomy, competence, and relatedness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The view that behavior is motivated by the need to reduce internal tension from unmet needs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Passive resignation that follows repeated exposure to uncontrollable events.

questions.explanation: Drive-reduction theory is best described as the view that behavior is motivated by the need to reduce internal tension from unmet needs. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Studying only to win a prize rather than out of interest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prioritizing food and safety before pursuing esteem and personal growth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reading a novel purely because it is enjoyable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Becoming worn down and prone to illness after prolonged, unrelenting stress.

questions.explanation: An example of Maslow's hierarchy of needs is: prioritizing food and safety before pursuing esteem and personal growth. In general, Maslow's hierarchy of needs refers to a theory ranking needs from basic physiological ones up to self-fulfillment. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the drop in intrinsic motivation after being rewarded for an already enjoyed activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: cortisol

choices.label: B
choices.text: distress

choices.label: C
choices.text: burnout

choices.label: D
choices.text: the overjustification effect

questions.explanation: This description matches the overjustification effect, which is the drop in intrinsic motivation after being rewarded for an already enjoyed activity. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the lateral hypothalamus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The idea that one's facial expressions can influence one's emotional experience.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A brain region whose activity triggers hunger.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A complex, unlearned behavior pattern shared across members of a species.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cumulative wear on the body produced by chronic stress.

questions.explanation: The lateral hypothalamus is best described as a brain region whose activity triggers hunger. The other options describe different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the James-Lange theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hormone that rises before meals and prompts a feeling of hunger.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Concluding one is afraid because one notices a racing heart and trembling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Feeling calm or afraid based first on judging whether a situation is threatening.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assessing whether one has the time and skills to handle a heavy workload.

questions.explanation: An example of the James-Lange theory is: concluding one is afraid because one notices a racing heart and trembling. In general, the James-Lange theory refers to the theory that emotion results from perceiving one's own bodily arousal. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the theory that a cognitive appraisal of a situation precedes and shapes emotion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Lazarus appraisal theory

choices.label: B
choices.text: ghrelin

choices.label: C
choices.text: primary appraisal

choices.label: D
choices.text: the facial feedback hypothesis

questions.explanation: This description matches the Lazarus appraisal theory, which is the theory that a cognitive appraisal of a situation precedes and shapes emotion. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes a stressor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any event or condition that places demands on a person and triggers stress.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cultural norms that govern how and when emotions may be expressed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Physical and emotional exhaustion resulting from prolonged occupational stress.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coping by acting directly to address the source of a stressor.

questions.explanation: A stressor is best described as any event or condition that places demands on a person and triggers stress. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the resistance stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Working extra hours because of a promised cash bonus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The body staying on high alert while managing an ongoing, drawn-out crisis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Birds building nests without ever being taught how.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Making a study schedule to tackle the stress of an upcoming exam.

questions.explanation: An example of the resistance stage is: the body staying on high alert while managing an ongoing, drawn-out crisis. In general, the resistance stage refers to the second stress stage, in which the body copes with continued stress. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the body's rapid physiological mobilization to confront or flee a threat?

choices.label: A
choices.text: secondary appraisal

choices.label: B
choices.text: the fight-or-flight response

choices.label: C
choices.text: an instinct

choices.label: D
choices.text: homeostasis

questions.explanation: This description matches the fight-or-flight response, which is the body's rapid physiological mobilization to confront or flee a threat. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes allostatic load?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coping by managing one's emotional response to a stressor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The cumulative wear on the body produced by chronic stress.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The three-stage pattern of the body's response to prolonged stress.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The first stress stage, in which the body mobilizes its fight-or-flight resources.

questions.explanation: Allostatic load is best described as the cumulative wear on the body produced by chronic stress. The other options describe different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of social support?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The body staying on high alert while managing an ongoing, drawn-out crisis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A hormone that rises under stress and raises available blood sugar.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Friends offering encouragement and practical help during a difficult time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An approaching exam deadline that creates mounting pressure.

questions.explanation: An example of social support is: friends offering encouragement and practical help during a difficult time. In general, social support refers to the comfort and resources provided by one's social network during stress. The other options illustrate different concepts.
